metadata.split_role: Expanded gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, parasitic, and remaining oddball bolete records; second half of former mushrooms-boletes-other.json.
metadata.notes: Split from former mushrooms-boletes-other.json in v4.2.90 to reduce large-file handling/static. Record bodies and order were preserved; each canonical slug still appears in exactly one base mushroom split file. Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. v4.2.81 added species-specific bolete notes/source links and simplified review reasons for selected records. v4.2.91: other-bolete review pass updated review flags using the 2-3 distinct-image rule. v4.2.92: removed stale seed/review/build notes from user-facing nested profile fields while preserving active review reasons. v4.2.93 reviewed the 11 remaining other-bolete Needs Review records, resolving records with adequate source/photo support and retaining only true blocked review reasons.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.distinct_image_rule: 2-3 image coverage means 2-3 genuinely different source images; thumbnails/detail/full variants or placeholders do not count.

v4_2_93_review_audit.scope: Remaining Needs Review records in other-bolete split files.
v4_2_93_review_audit.rule: Clear only when actual review reason is fixed; keep photo review when fewer than 2 genuinely distinct real images are available.

records.culinary_uses: Not emphasized as a food mushroom; better kept as a confusing look-alike/comparison entry.

mushroom_profile.summary: Shaggy Strobilomyces species close to Old Man of the Woods; field ID is tricky and spores are the reliable separator.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; eastern North America.
research_notes: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black. Separating it from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods can require spore characters.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.
pore_color: Whitish pores bruising reddish then black

records.edibility_detail: Treat conservatively; ID can be difficult without microscopy.
records.mushroom_card_note: Shaggy Strobilomyces species close to Old Man of the Woods; field ID is tricky and spores are the reliable separator.
records.field_identification: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black. Separating it from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods can require spore characters.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Often considered edible, but this guide treats it as a careful-ID species rather than a beginner mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
research_notes: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Edible only with confident ID; gilled-bolete separation can be subtle.
records.mushroom_card_note: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
records.field_identification: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Often treated as edible, but keep the ID conservative because gilled bolete concepts are shifting.

mushroom_profile.summary: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
staining: Usually little to no bluing, occasional bluing reported
research_notes: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Edible with strong ID; not a beginner shortcut mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
records.field_identification: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Not a high-value edible; better as an identification/comparison species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
staining: Variable; generally not a strong blue-stainer
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.

records.edibility_detail: Use cautiously; not treated as a prime table mushroom here.
records.mushroom_card_note: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
records.field_identification: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target; useful as a Great Lakes specialty ID species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Great Lakes-region oak bolete with sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that brown strongly, and nearly round spores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, often in Great Lakes oak settings.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: MushroomExpert emphasizes Great Lakes/oak habitat and Michigan suburban oak collections; Commons provides two distinct public images.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
image_review_reasons: No usable field-view photo is currently available in the app.
Replace placeholder with 2-3 genuinely different source photos when available.
records.mushroom_card_note: Great Lakes-region oak bolete with sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that brown strongly, and nearly round spores.
records.field_identification: Look for a sticky yellow-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface that bruises dark brown, a sheathing veil/ring zone low on the stem, oak association, and nearly round spores. Known especially from Great Lakes oak settings.
records.edibility_detail: ID/comparison entry, not a recommended edible in this guide.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: placeholder/no real image remains; keep in Needs Review until real distinct photos are found.
v4.2.93 added two distinct Commons images and resolved image coverage.
records.photo_subject: Two distinct Commons field images from Michigan/Great Lakes context; enough for coverage but still a specialist ID species.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Paragyrodon sphaerosporus
use_links.notes: Great Lakes/oak habitat, Michigan note, field traits, and spore traits.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food because it grows directly on Scleroderma earthballs, which are poisonous.
records.notes: Needs review: Needs more distinct photo coverage.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Parasitic Bolete. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Little to no staining; may stain dirty brown slowly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, when earthballs are present.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: Commons category has extensive Pseudoboletus parasiticus media; record now has three distinct field images.
Key field clue remains growth directly on Scleroderma earthballs.

records.general_notes: Useful as a caution/curiosity species because the growth habit is distinctive: a bolete growing on another fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Small yellow-brown bolete that grows directly from common earthballs (Scleroderma), usually the most important ID feature. Pores are yellow to olive and the stem is small and often curved.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Pseudoboletus parasiticus
use_links.notes: Large public media set and taxon category.

records.edibility_notes: The mushroom itself is sometimes described as edible, but this guide treats it as avoid/not recommended because of its poisonous earthball host.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added during the bolete expansion pass for the clean app; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food in this guide.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-growing Boletales oddball with a veined/merulioid yellow-orange fertile surface; not a normal cap-and-stem bolete target.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where observed.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: kept in Other lane because it is a wood-growing veined/merulioid Boletales oddball, not a classic cap-and-stem bolete.
Added additional Commons image and a field-guide style source link.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.mushroom_card_note: Wood-growing Boletales oddball with a veined/merulioid yellow-orange fertile surface; not a normal cap-and-stem bolete target.
records.field_identification: Look for a sessile or reduced-stem fruit body on dead/debarked conifer wood, with a fuzzy yellow cap surface and wavy, gill-like to veined yellow-orange underside rather than round bolete pores. Keep in the Other lane, not the main bolete lane.
records.edibility_detail: Keep as an identification/odd-form record, not a forage target.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: only one distinct real image is loaded; keep in Needs Review until at least 2 genuinely different photos are available.
v4.2.93 resolved image/source/UI placement review by adding a second Commons image, field-guide style source, and confirming Other lane placement.
records.photo_subject: Two distinct Commons field images; keep in Other rather than normal pored-bolete lane.

use_links.notes: Wood substrate and veined yellow-orange fertile surface.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Pseudomerulius
use_links.notes: Genus category with Pseudomerulius curtisii media.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target; the distinctive yellow veil makes it more useful for identification.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bright yellow-veiled bolete when young, often with reddish/orange cap tones and blue-green bruising on pores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; primarily with conifers, especially pine, hemlock, and spruce.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: MushroomExpert describes the bright yellow veil, blue-green bruising, and broad North American distribution; Commons has seven media files.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.mushroom_card_note: Bright yellow-veiled bolete when young, often with reddish/orange cap tones and blue-green bruising on pores.
records.field_identification: Young mushrooms have a powdery bright yellow veil over the pore surface and stem; older mushrooms show yellow veil remnants, reddish to orange cap colors, yellowish pores, and greenish-blue bruising.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid as a food target in this guide.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: placeholder/no real image remains; keep in Needs Review until real distinct photos are found.
v4.2.93 added distinct Commons images and resolved photo coverage.
records.photo_subject: Three distinct Commons field images showing the bright yellow-veiled bolete form.

records.culinary_uses: Not a target food mushroom; included for identification/comparison.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small sticky yellow-orange to reddish bolete with an unusually long, deeply reticulate stem; reported from Michigan but more southern/Appalachian in emphasis.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; recorded from Michigan to southeastern U.S.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: two distinct Commons/iNaturalist images added; kept as ID/comparison rather than a food target.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.mushroom_card_note: Small sticky yellow-orange to reddish bolete with an unusually long, deeply reticulate stem; reported from Michigan but more southern/Appalachian in emphasis.
records.field_identification: Check for sticky bright yellow/orange to reddish cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a striking long stem that is deeply ridged or lacerate-reticulate. Oak or mixed oak-pine association.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as ID/comparison until local confidence is stronger.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: placeholder/no real image remains; keep in Needs Review until real distinct photos are found.
v4.2.93 added two distinct Commons images and resolved photo coverage.
records.photo_subject: Two distinct Commons/iNaturalist images; enough for coverage, but retain conservative non-target status.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food; useful as a distinctive yellow-pored bolete and comparison species.
records.notes: Needs review: Needs real field photo / image coverage.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern oak-forest bolete with sticky pinkish-brown cap and bright yellow non-bruising pores; cap surface may taste sour.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: MushroomExpert places it in eastern North American oak forests; Commons provides several public images.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
records.mushroom_card_note: Eastern oak-forest bolete with sticky pinkish-brown cap and bright yellow non-bruising pores; cap surface may taste sour.
records.field_identification: Look for sticky pinkish-brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pore surface that does not bruise, white basal mycelium, and a slender reddish/yellowish stem. Mycorrhizal with oaks and other hardwoods in eastern North America.
records.edibility_detail: Occasional/niche use only; not a main target.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: placeholder/no real image remains; keep in Needs Review until real distinct photos are found.
v4.2.93 added distinct Commons images and resolved photo coverage.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food; included for odd wood-associated bolete recognition.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus tied to conifer wood and possibly Phaeolus schweinitzii; keep as an oddball ID record.
research_notes: Reviewed in v4.2.93: Commons provides field and pore-surface images; retained as wood-associated oddball ID/comparison record.

manual_review_reasons: Needs real field photo / image coverage
boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.mushroom_card_note: Wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus tied to conifer wood and possibly Phaeolus schweinitzii; keep as an oddball ID record.
records.field_identification: Yellow-brown cap, yellow pore surface, wood association, and possible relationship with the dyer’s polypore Phaeolus schweinitzii are the useful clues. Compare with B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.
